nativeRange.habitat: Deep coastal slopes and shallow muddy estuaries; reported on rubble and patch reef margins, often mixing with other fairy and flasher wrasses
nativeRange.biotope: Western Pacific reef slopes (notably around Sumatra), typically reported between roughly 10–35 m

diet.notes: Documented as a zooplanktivore in the wild, foraging above the substrate. In aquariums, generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafood, and high-quality marine flake or pellet foods. Multiple small feedings per day are commonly advised, especially for newly imported specimens.

avoidWith: other male fairy wrasses in the same tank
similarly coloured Cirrhilabrus or Paracheilinus males

summary: The Filamentosus Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus filamentosus) is a small Western Pacific reef fish named for the male's elongated dorsal-fin filament. Generally considered peaceful and reef-safe, it is well suited to mature mid-sized reef aquariums — provided the tank has a tightly fitted lid.
careNotes: Cirrhilabrus filamentosus is best known for the male's dramatically elongated dorsal-fin filament, which can approach or exceed body length in full display. The species is documented from the Western Pacific around Indonesia — with Sumatra frequently cited in the trade — at depths of roughly 10–35 m on coastal slopes and patch reef rubble; some retailers describe the origin more broadly as 'Indian Ocean' or 'Indo-Pacific', which appears to overstate the documented range. Generally considered peaceful and reef-safe — coral and ornamental-invert nipping is not commonly reported — but males have been documented to dominate or harass other male Cirrhilabrus and Paracheilinus, so keeping only one male per tank is strongly advised. Like most fairy wrasses, this species tends to be a notable jumper, and a tightly fitted lid or eurobrace is strongly recommended. It is best kept as a single male, or as a male with one or more females added simultaneously to form a harem; introducing females after the male is established tends to provoke aggression. Individuals are protogynous hermaphrodites — meaning they are born female and can transition to male — and males display heightened nuptial coloration during courtship.
breedingNotes: Captive spawning is rarely reported. In the wild, the species shows distinct pairing and harem structure with a dominant male over groups of females and juveniles; replicating this social structure may encourage nuptial display behavior in well-established reef systems.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Originally-designated species URL (/products/filamentosus-fairy-wrasse) returned 404; LiveAquaria markets this species under the trade name 'Whip Fin Fairy Wrasse'. Used for water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), 55 gal minimum tank, 'easy' care level, peaceful temperament, reef-safe status, carnivore diet, and jumper warning.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), 8 cm TL max length, 10–35 m depth range, Western Pacific (Indonesia) distribution, zooplanktivorous diet, harem/pair behaviour. IUCN: Data Deficient.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog — Fairy Wrasse Care
additional.notes: Genus-level care reference. Confirms 3–5 year captive lifespan, protogynous hermaphroditism, haremic social structure, high jumping/escape risk, and male-on-male aggression. Suggests 55 gal solo and 110 gal harem minimums — used to justify biasing tank size above the retailer floor.

additional.name: Aqua Integrated
additional.notes: Independent retailer corroboration: 4 in adult size, 50 gal minimum tank, 74–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, beginner-to-intermediate care level, peaceful/reef-safe, known jumper.
